title: Middle East flare-up to boost supply chain stress
author: Sally Li
text:
RESEARCH BRIEFING

24 Jul 2026

Middle East flare-up to boost supply chain stress

We expect a more drawn-out Middle East conflict, with a long-term reduction in the level of shipping through the Strait.

The recent flare-up in US-Iran tensions has led to the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz again and we now expect a more drawn-out conflict, with a long-term reduction in the level of shipping through the Strait.

What you will learn:

Supply-chain stress will increase in the near-term and remain at a more elevated level than previously anticipated. Acute supply pressures will remain more concentrated in Asia, with smaller and more price-driven impacts in Europe and the US.

Sulphuric acid prices have continued to increase. A heavy reliance on Middle Eastern exports, compounded by China’s export ban, has driven especially sharp increases in Asia.

Refined oil product prices have also crept up since the renewal in US-Iran tensions, particularly for diesel and jet fuel.

Despite the disruption, there is still a significant degree of strategic inventory in the US and Germany, meaning the global helium market still retains solid buffers.
